reason: The item cleanly tests scope-limited validation reasoning: peak-pressure agreement supports a narrow claim, while missed oscillatory dynamics constrain applicability near the opening and raise mixture-sensitivity concerns. Decoys are generally single-fault and engineering-plausible, forcing experts to separate quantity-specific validation from broader hazard or dynamics generalizations. Slight boundary fuzziness remains around whether a single off-stoichiometric point suffices for “validated” language and how strongly oscillation miss maps to all dynamic loading use-cases, limiting top-tier discriminative strength.

The item cleanly targets boundary-aware engineering judgment about admissible safety-use claims from a documented 650 K underprediction with moderate fit and large dispersion, and the correct options are tightly anchored to those stated performance metrics. Decoys are largely single-fault and plausible (overreliance on R², cross-temperature overgeneralization, and mechanistic over-claim), yielding meaningful discrimination, though D5 is capped because some options remain somewhat easy for experts due to overt absolutist wording (e.g., “all other temperatures,” “for all engineering safety purposes”).

The prompt tightly anchors the needed response to two explicit scope/validation gaps (geometry/release-profile dependence of pressures, and CFD’s inability to capture oscillatory dynamics), which are directly diagnostic of boundary-aware safety reasoning. It strongly differentiates experts who can separate peak-pressure agreement from broader model validity and who won’t transfer partly confined results to other confinement regimes, while keeping the task focused on engineering consequences rather than literature trivia.
